summary: I was born in Lynch, Kentucky in 1949, and I grew up in a coal camp called Number Three there. Lynch was one of the Tri-Cities along with Cumberland and Benham, and it was home to the U.S. Steel Mine. I attended West Main School in Lynch, which was part of the Lynch Independent School District, and I have many memories of the close-knit community and the pool room where we socialized. My family lived in company housing, and when my father retired from mining, we had to move to Cumberland.

I was born in Lynch, Kentucky, and at that time the hospital was called Notre Dame Hospital. It has since changed names and is now known as the Meridzo Center or Solomon's Porch. This hospital was the place of my birth in 1949, marking the beginning of my life in the Lynch community.

The hospital where I was born in Lynch, Kentucky, was originally called Notre Dame Hospital but is now known as the Meridzo Center or Solomon's Porch. This change happened sometime after my birth in 1949. The Meridzo Center represents the modern identity of the place that was once Notre Dame Hospital.

Solomon's Porch is the current name of the hospital in Lynch, Kentucky, where I was born in 1949. It was originally called Notre Dame Hospital, but over time the name changed to Solomon's Porch or Meridzo Center. This place marks the beginning of my life and the healthcare facility serving our community.

Cumberland, Kentucky was the trade area among the Tri-Cities, which included Lynch and Benham. After my father retired from mining, we had to relocate from Lynch to Cumberland because we lived in company housing. I attended school in Cumberland after the move, while my sister and brother stayed in Lynch to finish their schooling.

Benham, Kentucky was one of the Tri-Cities along with Lynch and Cumberland. It was home to the International Harvester Mine, and like Lynch, it had its own coal camp and school system. I remember that Benham had a youth center similar to the pool room in Lynch, where kids gathered and parents served as chaperones to keep order.

International Harvester operated the mine in Benham, Kentucky, which was one of the Tri-Cities coal camps alongside Lynch and Cumberland. Their coal camp and community were very similar to those of U.S. Steel in Lynch, and the towns literally ran into each other. This company played a big role in the local economy and community life during my childhood.

U.S. Steel operated the coal mine in Lynch, Kentucky, where I was born and raised. My father was a retired coal miner who worked for U.S. Steel, and our family lived in a company camp called Number Three. The company’s presence shaped much of our community and daily life, including the housing and school systems.

The United Mine Workers of America, or UMWA, was an important organization in our community. I remember that my sister was chosen as a queen candidate for the UMWA during the early years of school integration in Lynch. The UMWA played a role in community events and represented the coal miners and their families.

My brother enlisted in the United States Army after finishing high school. He worked in the coal mines at Lynch before joining the Army, as did my husband. The Army was a path he chose following his time in the mining community.

Gap Branch was an area within the coal mines near Lynch where my brother and husband worked. They were assigned to Portal Thirty-five, which was one of the numbered sections of the mine. This place was part of the mining operations that supported our community’s livelihood.

I attended West Main School in Lynch, Kentucky, from first through ninth grade. It was originally called Lynch Colored School during my mother’s time but was renamed West Main by the time I went there. The school was part of the Lynch Independent School District and served Black students until integration around 1964 or 1965.

On Sundays, after church and Sunday school, we would attend the Baptist Training Union, or BTU, which kept us busy in the evenings. It was an important part of our religious and social life growing up in Lynch. Attending church activities like BTU was mandatory if we wanted to participate in other social events like the pool room.

The Benham Youth Center was similar to the pool room in Lynch where kids gathered to socialize, dance, and have fun. Parents served as chaperones to maintain order, and the police station was nearby to ensure discipline. It was a place where the youth could gather safely under adult supervision in Benham.

The Lynch Independent School District was the school system that served our community in Lynch, Kentucky. West Main School, which I attended, was part of this district. The district maintained separate schools for Black and white students until integration occurred around 1964 or 1965.
